exercises.title: Gini impurity
exercises.prompt: Implement Gini impurity and compute it for a pure node (all one class) and a 50/50 mixed node. Explain what a split is trying to do to impurity.
exercises.hint: Gini = 1 − Σ p_c². Pure = 0, max for two classes = 0.5.

exercises.title: Best split by information gain
exercises.prompt: Given a feature and labels, find the threshold that maximizes information gain (parent impurity minus weighted child impurity). Confirm it picks the cleanest separation.
exercises.hint: For each candidate threshold, split into left/right, compute weighted Gini, and gain = parent − weighted children.

exercises.title: Why a forest beats a tree
exercises.prompt: Explain how a random forest reduces variance versus a single deep tree, covering both bootstrap sampling and feature randomization. Why does averaging many *decorrelated* trees help, and what's the catch with MDI feature importance?
exercises.hint: A single deep tree is low-bias/high-variance. Averaging reduces variance only if the trees' errors are decorrelated — hence bagging + random feature subsets.
exercises.solution:
A single deep tree fits training data almost perfectly — **low bias, high variance** (small data changes produce very different trees). Averaging many trees reduces variance, but only if their errors are **decorrelated**; identical trees would just reproduce the same mistake. Random forests force decorrelation two ways:

1. **Bootstrap sampling (bagging):** each tree trains on a random resample-with-replacement of the data, so they see different examples.
2. **Feature randomization:** at each split, only a random subset of features is considered, so trees can't all key off the same dominant feature.

Averaging these decorrelated trees keeps the low bias while slashing variance — that's why forests (and boosted trees) dominate tabular ML.

**The MDI catch:** mean-decrease-in-impurity importance is biased toward high-cardinality and continuous features (they offer more split points, so they accumulate spurious impurity reductions). For trustworthy importances, prefer **permutation importance** — shuffle a feature's values and measure the real drop in validation performance.
